jokes.text: The aerobics/water aerobics class devolves into a full dramatic reenactment of The Sandlot's pool scene, with Abbi and her elderly students acting out Squints faking drowning to kiss Wendy Peffercorn
jokes.setup: Abbi is teaching what appears to be a senior water aerobics class, doing normal instructor patter
jokes.punchline: The class has fully committed to an elaborate roleplay of the Sandlot pool scene — 'He's a dirty liar,' 'One more time and I am leaving you in the pool to die' — completely earnest and detailed
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels — the absurdity of elderly people doing an elaborate Sandlot reenactment, Abbi's completely sincere participation, and the sustained commitment to the bit. The specific detail of 'three weeks ago' implies this is a recurring problem with one student, which deepens the absurdity.

jokes.text: A creepy old male student to Abbi: 'I know how to do it without popping your cherry. Your husband will never suspect on your wedding night.'
jokes.setup: Class has just ended, Abbi calls out students warmly
jokes.punchline: An elderly male student approaches Abbi with an extremely inappropriate come-on framed as helpful advice, referencing her virginity and wedding night
jokes.explanation: Smart callback to the Squints bit — the joke suggests this man genuinely IS Squints, a lifelong creep, and the wedding night comment is shockingly specific and gross in the best way. The 'I know your granddaughter / I'm texting her right now' and 'elder abuse!' beat that follows makes this a sustained cringe sequence.

jokes.text: Ilana shoots a basketball into a trash can from across the office, knocks something over, and announces herself with 'She shoots, she scores!'
jokes.setup: We cut to the Deals, Deals, Deals office; Ilana has not been told not to come in
jokes.punchline: She enters dramatically with a half-court shot, instantly breaks something, apologizes twice, completely unbothered
jokes.explanation: Functional entrance gag that instantly establishes Ilana's chaos energy in the workplace. The double 'sorry sorry' with zero change in demeanor is good character work. Nothing extraordinary but cleanly executed.

jokes.text: Ilana's work email is IlanaWexler@MindMyVagina.com and her boss Todd refuses to use it
jokes.setup: Todd tells Ilana he emailed her not to come in
jokes.punchline: Her email address is IlanaWexler@MindMyVagina.com — a domain she takes seriously ('I'm legal, Todd') while her boss refuses to engage with it on principle
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. The email address is both a perfect character detail (Ilana would absolutely have this email, would use it professionally, and would defend it with 'I'm legal') and a compression of the entire Ilana/Todd power dynamic. Todd's principled refusal to acknowledge it is the perfect straight-man response. The specificity — a whole domain, not just a gmail — makes it funnier. Could ONLY be Ilana. Earns its 8+.

jokes.text: Todd: 'I e-mailed you last night in a very large, bold font, saying not to come in today.' Ilana: 'Did you e-mail IlanaWexler@MindMyVagina.com?' Todd: 'I refuse to use that e-mail address, you know that.'
jokes.setup: Todd is furious Ilana showed up when he told her not to; he sent an email
jokes.punchline: Her professional email address is IlanaWexler@MindMyVagina.com and she uses it as a genuine deflection — and Todd has apparently been refusing to use it for some time
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The email address itself is funny, but what elevates it is the layered implication: (1) Ilana has had this email long enough that Todd has developed a firm policy about it; (2) she genuinely uses it as her professional contact; (3) she invokes it as a legitimate legal defense ('I'm legal, Todd'). The phrase 'I'm legal, Todd' as a follow-up is perfect economy. This is character comedy at its best — only Ilana, only this show.

jokes.text: Ilana pitches her app ideas to Todd as 'a bazillion app ideas that some crusty old white douche would jizz his little pantsuit for'
jokes.setup: Todd explains there's an important investor coming and they need funding
jokes.punchline: Ilana's response is enthusiastic agreement that is also maximally inappropriate — 'jizz his little pantsuit' combines gender-scrambled imagery with unearned confidence
jokes.explanation: The 'pantsuit' detail does the heavy lifting — it's a gender-mixed image that also signals Ilana's assumption the investor is a man in woman's clothing, which becomes ironic when the investor turns out to be a woman. Good Ilana voice.

jokes.text: Ilana is wearing what is clearly a dog hoodie — it has ear holes in the hood — and insists it's from 'the dog owner's section of American Apparel'
jokes.setup: Todd tells Ilana to go home; she's wearing something weird
jokes.punchline: She's wearing a hoodie designed for dogs, not dog owners — it has ear holes where dog ears would poke through, and a leash hole in the back
jokes.explanation: The visual is inherently funny — Ilana is wearing a dog hoodie with her head through the neck hole and the ear holes flopping on top of her head. The rationalization ('dog owner's section') is great, and Todd's Socratic deconstruction ('Why would they do that in a hood? A hood is to cover a head. Is there a pocket on the back?') escalates it perfectly. Ilana's resigned 'Not the first time this has happened to me. Three other times.' is a beautiful closer.

jokes.text: 'Not the first time this has happened to me. Three other times.'
jokes.setup: Todd points out the dog hoodie has a leash hole and ear holes
jokes.punchline: Ilana casually reveals she has accidentally worn dog clothing at least three other times — delivered completely straight
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy joke. 'Three other times' does so much work — it's not a one-off mistake, it's a pattern, and she's aware of the pattern and unbothered. The deadpan delivery implied here is crucial. Only Ilana could have a running habit of accidentally wearing dog clothing. This is the kind of throwaway that becomes a favorite.

jokes.text: Trey tells Abbi he gets why she needed to decorate her locker: 'I need to curate my space and, it's like, a vision board, you... You know what I mean? Because it's like, I put my stuff in it.'
jokes.setup: Trey is validating Abbi's locker decorating and explains his own philosophy
jokes.punchline: His 'vision board' rationale dissolves into 'I put my stuff in it' — the most basic possible description of a locker, delivered with deep conviction
jokes.explanation: Classic Trey joke — he begins with pseudo-profound gym-bro language ('curate my space,' 'vision board') and lands on something utterly banal ('I put my stuff in it'), said with complete sincerity. Good character work.

jokes.text: Trey compliments Abbi smelling like chlorine and describes his locker decoration philosophy as a 'vision board' — then trails off completely
jokes.setup: Trey approaches Abbi post-class with what seems like flirtation
jokes.punchline: His profundity completely collapses: 'It's like, I need to curate my space and, it's like, a vision board, you... You know what I mean? Because it's like, I put my stuff in it.' The sentence goes nowhere.
jokes.explanation: Trey is a great recurring gag — deeply sincere, extremely dim. The vision board explanation dissolving into 'I put my stuff in it' is a nice portrait of someone reaching for depth they don't have.

jokes.text: Trey and Abbi simultaneously say 'Zen out' and then do an extended jinx ritual ('Jinx! / Jinx! / I was gonna say jinx. / Oh. We're buds. / Zen out jinx!')
jokes.setup: Trey and Abbi are bonding over their shared locker/Axe body spray philosophy
jokes.punchline: The jinx spiral escalates past normal jinx protocol into a mutually delighted extended ritual that reveals how much Trey wants to be Abbi's friend
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the escalation — a simple jinx becomes a whole thing because Trey needs it to be. His 'Oh. We're buds.' is touching and funny simultaneously. Good sustained beat.

jokes.text: Trey: 'Oh, Abbi. I love you.' Abbi: 'I... I love you...' Trey: 'You do too? Yeah.' followed by him calling her 'my little cupcake' and immediately pivoting to 'My supplements. All hands on deck.'
jokes.setup: Jinx ritual has Trey feeling the warmth of new friendship
jokes.punchline: He tells Abbi he loves her; she's caught off guard and reciprocates awkwardly; he immediately deflates the intimacy by being called to his supplements, treating it all as equally important
jokes.explanation: The 'You do too? Yeah.' is a perfect Trey line — he's so emotionally uncomplicated that Abbi's confused return of 'I love you' reads to him as pure mutual feeling. Then 'my supplements, all hands on deck' immediately undercuts any emotional weight. The juxtaposition of sincerity and total obliviousness is Trey's core joke.

jokes.text: Trey offers Abbi a private changing room 'if you'd be more comfortable.' Abbi: 'No, I'm totally at ease.' Beat. 'Where is it?'
jokes.setup: Trey is clearly noticing Abbi is uncomfortable changing near him and others; he offers her a graceful out
jokes.punchline: Abbi insists she's fine — two seconds of silence — then immediately asks where the private room is, completely undermining her denial
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured beat. The comedy is entirely in the gap between 'No, I'm totally at ease' and 'Where is it?' — the world's fastest self-contradiction. Perfect comedic timing in two lines. This is the kind of joke that's simple in concept but requires precise execution to land, and the transcript suggests it does. Abbi's self-defeating pride is core character work.

jokes.text: Nicole enters in sharp business attire. Ilana: 'Nic, fierce shoulder pads. Date night?' Nicole: 'No, the investor's coming. Todd just told you.' Ilana: 'Woof, baby brain.'
jokes.setup: Nicole comes in dressed professionally for the investor
jokes.punchline: Ilana misreads professional attire as date night wear, then when corrected (about something that applies only to her), immediately diagnoses Nicole with 'baby brain'
jokes.explanation: Ilana projecting her own memory failure onto Nicole as 'baby brain' is a sharp reversal. The 'Are you pregnant? No, are you? No.' follow-up is a nice economy capper on the bit.

jokes.text: 'Are you pregnant?' / 'No, are you?' / 'No.'
jokes.setup: Ilana says 'baby brain' to Nicole after forgetting the investor news herself
jokes.punchline: Nicole asks if Ilana is pregnant; Ilana deflects with 'no, are you?'; Nicole says no; total silence/stalemate, no resolution
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the pure deadlock — two women doing an accidental Mexican standoff of mutual pregnancy accusations, ending in mutual 'no's and an implied shrug. Economical, mild chuckle, nothing more.

jokes.text: The investor Elizabeth Carlton walks in and Ilana greets her with 'Fuh-law-less bish!'
jokes.setup: Todd is introducing the important investor to his employees; stakes are high
jokes.punchline: Ilana opens with an extremely casual/street greeting normally reserved for close friends, directed at a stranger investor who is there to write a large check
jokes.explanation: The cringe comes from the mismatch of register — this is a professional moment and Ilana immediately treats Elizabeth Carlton like a close friend she ran into at brunch. The fact that Carlton seems almost charmed rather than offended is an interesting twist.

jokes.text: Elizabeth: 'This will foster a sense of equality.' Ilana: 'And you can call me Ilana. This will foster a sense of, um... You knowing my name.'
jokes.setup: Carlton invites everyone to call her Elizabeth to foster equality
jokes.punchline: Ilana mirrors the formal phrasing ('This will foster a sense of...') and then delivers a completely tautological non-reason — 'you knowing my name' — as if it's equally profound
jokes.explanation: Elegant structural joke. Ilana apes Carlton's formal register ('This will foster a sense of...') and then fills in the blank with pure absurdist bathos: 'you knowing my name.' The gap between the setup's implied profundity and the punchline's inanity is perfectly calibrated. The hesitant 'um...' before the landing implies Ilana is genuinely trying to construct a reason and this is the best she can do. One of the episode's strongest joke constructions.

jokes.text: Ilana clocks Carlton's blazer: 'TJ Maxx. 70% off. Maxxinista! Ay, Chihuahua!' followed by 'I don't know if I want to be her or be in here.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is supposed to be professionally impressing the investor
jokes.punchline: She pivots to fan-girling over Carlton's discount blazer, using the TJ Maxx marketing slogan, exclaiming 'Ay, Chihuahua,' and then confessing sexual/admiring confusion
jokes.explanation: 'I don't know if I want to be her or be in here' is a very Ilana construction — casual bisexual admiration and fashion aspiration treated as equally weighted, expressed without filter to a business contact. The TJ Maxx branding reclamation is a nice specific detail.

jokes.text: Ilana asks Carlton to rub her back 'real fast and hard' after getting a chill
jokes.setup: Ilana has just announced she doesn't know if she wants to 'be in' Carlton; Carlton seems bemused
jokes.punchline: Ilana physically requests back-rubbing from the investor as a natural next step in their relationship, 10 seconds after meeting her
jokes.explanation: Functionally funny — part of the Ilana-meets-Carlton cringe sequence. 'Real fast and hard' has an obvious double meaning that the show is aware of. Not deep but consistent character work.

jokes.text: Soulstice Games announcement — employees are surprised but Trey is fully committed: 'I remember my first time. I was 23 and I had just found out I was allergic to cashews.'
jokes.setup: Trey announces the Soulstice Games with great fanfare; others seem confused/unenthused
jokes.punchline: His nostalgic 'I remember my first time' is set up to be about the games but is completed with an entirely irrelevant allergy discovery — the allergy is the memory, not the event
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection. 'I remember my first time' strongly implies Soulstice Games nostalgia; the cashew allergy is so orthogonal that it takes a moment to register. But it's also perfect Trey character work — his life memories are organized around body/health milestones rather than conventional emotional ones. Clean setup, unexpected but logical-in-character landing. Excellent.

jokes.text: Abbi admits: 'In high school, people used to call me Tonya Harding.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is warning Trey she gets too competitive and might not be good for team-building games
jokes.punchline: The Tonya Harding nickname implies she was so competitive she was potentially violence-adjacent — this is an absurdly extreme benchmark for a recreational gym event
jokes.explanation: Strong character establishment joke that also seeds the episode's A-plot conflict. The callback potential (she DOES go full Tonya Harding) makes this a smart piece of foreshadowing delivered as a joke. Quotable.

jokes.text: Trey: 'There's no I in Soulstice.' Abbi: 'But there's...' Trey: 'Well, yes, there is, yes. I've actually been trying to get them to change it to a Y for a long time.'
jokes.setup: Trey uses the 'no I in team' construction to encourage Abbi about the Soulstice Games
jokes.punchline: There IS an I in Soulstice, and Trey knows this, and has been lobbying to fix it — meaning he's been using a broken inspirational saying for a long time anyway
jokes.explanation: A great subversion of the 'no I in team' sports cliché. The joke has a second layer: Trey has clearly been corrected on this before (he acknowledges it mid-sentence), yet still deploys the line anyway. The detail that he's lobbying to rename the company 'Soulstce' to fix the grammatical problem with his own inspirational saying is genuinely clever.

jokes.text: Trey: 'You still got time to shave your pubes if you need to lose that last L-B.'
jokes.setup: Trey is announcing the weigh-in before the Soulstice Games
jokes.punchline: He offers pubic shaving as a legitimate weight-loss strategy before weigh-in — a real but extremely intimate suggestion delivered casually to the whole room
jokes.explanation: One of the better Trey jokes in the episode. The specificity ('that last L-B') paired with the extreme intimacy of the solution (pubic shaving) is a perfect gym-bro character joke. 'L-B' for pound is an additional funny detail — the abbreviation sounds more clinical and makes the surrounding weirdness worse. Abbi's 'There's where the fucking pubes come from' reaction beat is the cherry on top.

jokes.text: Abbi's reaction: 'There's where the fucking pubes come from.'
jokes.setup: Trey exits after the pube-shaving suggestion; Abbi is left standing there
jokes.punchline: Abbi's line implies she has been finding mysterious pubes around the gym and has now solved the mystery — said with exhausted recognition rather than shock
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. The line implies an entire unseen backstory — Abbi has been finding unexplained pubic hairs around the gym and has been puzzling over their origin. The 'there's WHERE they come from' phrasing treats solving a mystery about loose pubes with the same resignation as learning where a leak in the ceiling comes from. The fact that she seems RELIEVED rather than disgusted is perfect character work. Economy is near-perfect. One of the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: Elizabeth Carlton interrogating the company's social media: 'Our company hasn't tweeted in six days. What's worse, it's just a link. No voice, no copy.'
jokes.setup: Carlton is doing a serious business review
jokes.punchline: Her VC-speak analysis of their tweet quality ('no voice, no copy') is extremely precise and very funny in the context of a tiny deals company
jokes.explanation: Mild satire of corporate social media speak. Functions as setup for the Salad Fingers bit more than being a joke itself.

jokes.text: Elizabeth Carlton's boardroom analysis: 'Exactly. Nothing. We have to rise above the fray.' — delivered after asking what makes Deals, Deals, Deals different from Groupon
jokes.setup: Carlton asks what differentiates the company; presumably no one answers
jokes.punchline: She supplies her own brutal answer: nothing. Then pivots immediately to solutions
jokes.explanation: The '...Exactly. Nothing.' construction is well-executed corporate satire. Carlton's unsentimental self-awareness is funny against the general chaos of the office. Solid but not standout.

jokes.text: Ilana explains what she finds viral online: 'Salad Fingers... It's this guy with romaine lettuce for fingers, and he rubs up all on this spoon like... Salad fingers.' Then describes it as 'early Randomcore.'
jokes.setup: Carlton asks what Ilana would click on, to understand viral content
jokes.punchline: Ilana recommends Salad Fingers — the actual creepy Flash animation — and coins the micro-genre 'early Randomcore' with complete critical authority
jokes.explanation: Salad Fingers is a real and genuinely strange early internet artifact, and Ilana presenting it with total authority to a businesswoman in a boardroom context is funny. 'Early Randomcore' is brilliant genre-naming — it treats 2004 internet weirdness as a serious aesthetic movement. The joke is a time capsule and a character revelation simultaneously.

jokes.text: Ilana references 'End of Ze World' and 'the Aicha kid' — the white kid with 'crispy gelled hair' doing a choreographed dance with a bean bag — then sings a fragment
jokes.setup: Ilana is trying to identify viral content for Elizabeth
jokes.punchline: She keeps reaching further back into early 2000s internet obscurity, culminating in a half-remembered description of a real viral video complete with physical detail ('crispy gelled hair') and a performed fragment
jokes.explanation: The escalating obscurity of Ilana's internet references is the sustained joke here, and it works because the references are real — this isn't invented. Her performing the song is peak Ilana commitment. The joke rewards viewers who recognize the specific early internet era being referenced.

jokes.text: Ilana references 'The Aicha kid' — describes him as 'that white kid, skinny, crispy gelled hair' who does a choreographed dance with a bean bag — then performs the song herself
jokes.setup: Ilana is listing her catalog of viral content expertise for the investor
jokes.punchline: She performs the Aicha dance (a real early internet meme) in a boardroom while making a business case, completely committed, then is bewildered no one else knows it
jokes.explanation: The physical performance elevates this beyond just a reference joke. Ilana's genuine confusion that no one shares her encyclopedic early-internet knowledge, paired with her unashamed performance in a corporate meeting, is pure character. The generation gap 'How old are you guys?' turn is nicely self-aware.

jokes.text: Carlton: 'I have no idea what you're saying. And I love it.' Then she assigns Ilana to run the Twitter account.
jokes.setup: Todd and everyone else are horrified by Ilana's boardroom behavior; the obvious outcome would be Ilana getting removed
jokes.punchline: Carlton — the powerful investor — is charmed and rewards Ilana's chaos with a promotion
jokes.explanation: Good structural subversion — the expected result (Ilana gets thrown out) is inverted (Ilana gets promoted by the very person she was most threatening to impress). Todd's horror is the real punchline. Carlton's 'I have no idea what you're saying. And I love it.' is quotable and suggests a whole side character.

jokes.text: Abbi psyching herself up alone before the Soulstice Games: 'You got this, you're cool. Be cool. Just be fucking cool, man.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has confessed to Tonya Harding tendencies; she's alone before the event
jokes.punchline: Her self-talk is so desperate and specific that it undercuts any pretense of actually being cool — the triple 'cool' is itself uncool
jokes.explanation: Standard but well-executed 'trying too hard' comedy. The word 'man' at the end of 'just be fucking cool, man' is a nice touch — she's coaching herself like a disappointed older brother. Good Abbi character beat.

jokes.text: Abbi calls Ilana and asks if she can tweet one of Ilana's 'before' wax pics. Ilana: 'Which one? Neck?'
jokes.setup: Abbi calls Ilana mid-event to ask permission for a tweet involving a wax pic
jokes.punchline: Ilana has multiple 'before' wax photos categorized by body part, and 'neck' is offered first — implying neck waxing is a category she tracks
jokes.explanation: The comedy is entirely in 'Neck?' — the implication being that Ilana's wax archive is organized and extensive enough that she can't determine which one is meant without a body-part qualifier. Classic Ilana body-positive/boundary-free characterization delivered in one word.

jokes.text: Ilana clocks that Abbi is at a competitive event from a phone call: 'I hear your teeth grinding through the phone. You're at a competitive event, aren't you?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to hide that she's at the Soulstice Games; Ilana calls to discuss the tweet
jokes.punchline: Ilana diagnoses Abbi via auditory teeth-grinding detection — she knows Abbi's competitive symptoms so intimately she can identify them over the phone
jokes.explanation: Strong friendship-intimacy joke. The teeth-grinding detail is so specific it's clearly a documented Abbi symptom that Ilana has catalogued. This is the show at its best — jokes that simultaneously establish character dynamics and are funny in isolation. 'I hear your teeth grinding through the phone' is excellent.

jokes.text: 'How dare you lie to your wife? I hear your teeth grinding through the phone. You're at a competitive event, aren't you?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is lying about her whereabouts
jokes.punchline: Ilana calls Abbi her 'wife,' uses hyper-dramatic accusatory language ('How dare you'), and diagnoses tooth grinding through the phone — all delivered as normal relationship behavior
jokes.explanation: The Abbi-Ilana relationship coded as marriage is a consistent show joke and this is a good instance of it. Ilana's diagnostic abilities ('I hear your teeth grinding') are a nice absurdist touch.

jokes.text: Ilana invokes: 'Ab-bi! August 4th. 2002. Camp Kweebec. Red Rover.' Abbi: 'I remember, Ilana. I remember everyone being so fucking jealous 'cause I beat them so fucking hard.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is trying to convince Abbi that her competitive instinct is both known and acceptable; she references a formative incident
jokes.punchline: Abbi doesn't just 'remember' — she remembers with vivid, proud detail about how jealous everyone was, confirming she's been carrying this victory for 13 years
jokes.explanation: Rich friendship-history joke. The specificity of the date/camp/game implies this is a documented Abbi incident that Ilana keeps in her mental files to deploy when needed. Abbi's response reveals she has been dining on the memory of this Red Rover victory for over a decade, which makes the whole episode's competitive arc feel organic. This is also a setup for the episode's final joke.

jokes.text: 'I'm at the park where that dude sold my other sandal.'
jokes.setup: Abbi asks where Ilana is
jokes.punchline: Ilana locates herself entirely by reference to a previous misfortune involving a sandal-selling stranger — a totally specific and weird NYC coordinate that implies a whole backstory
jokes.explanation: Excellent Ilana joke. 'That dude sold my other sandal' — the word 'other' does critical work, implying this was not a unique event (she has a dude who sold ONE sandal, implying others), or that this was a sequential sale. The fact that this is her directional landmark in NYC is perfect world-building. One of the episode's most quotable and character-specific lines.

jokes.text: Musical sting: '♪ I shit ♪' — a brief original(?) jingle that plays as a scene transition
jokes.setup: Scene transition from Abbi at the Soulstice Games to the Deals, Deals, Deals office
jokes.punchline: The musical sting for the scene is just someone singing 'I shit' — a recurring audio gag in this episode
jokes.explanation: The recurring '♪ I shit ♪' sting is a Broad City-style absurdist musical gag — it appears multiple times in the episode at seemingly random transitions. The economy is perfect (two words) and the commitment to replaying it straight as a structural joke is distinctive. Context (colonics episode subplot?) makes it a running gag.

jokes.text: Nicole enters the lunch meeting saying she spearheaded the 'menu binder project' then immediately excuses herself for a 'cleanse'
jokes.setup: The investor meeting is continuing; Nicole tries to appear competent and proactive
jokes.punchline: Her one contribution (the menu binder) is immediately undercut by her leaving to handle cleanse-related matters, making 'I spearheaded the menu binder project' land as absurdly low-stakes self-promotion
jokes.explanation: Solid workplace satire — 'I spearheaded the menu binder project' as a brag is a good joke about corporate self-promotion culture. The immediate cleanse exit is a nice deadpan follow-through.

jokes.text: Nicole/Ilana runs into the bathroom to hide/help; Ilana opens the stall as a tour: 'I just wanted to show you the bathroom, myself, personally.' Carlton: 'Thanks, I'm familiar.'
jokes.setup: Something has gone wrong in the cleanse situation and Ilana is trying to cover; Carlton enters the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Ilana pretends she's conducting a voluntary bathroom tour, presenting the room as if Carlton hasn't seen a bathroom before
jokes.explanation: Carlton's 'Thanks, I'm familiar' is the best part — the dry, total disinterest in engaging with Ilana's cover story. 'Myself, personally' as a verbal tic is good Ilana redundancy.

jokes.text: Ilana offers Carlton free sanitary napkins: 'If you need sanitary napkins or similar products, we give those away free here.' Carlton: 'I understand.' Ilana: 'My mom spotted well into her 60s.' Todd: 'Thanks, Todd.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is improvising a cover for whatever is happening in the bathroom stall
jokes.punchline: She pivots to oversharing her mother's menstrual history to a business investor, then Todd undercuts himself by thanking himself
jokes.explanation: The 'my mom spotted well into her 60s' overshare is characteristically Ilana — personal gynecological family history deployed as small talk. Todd's inexplicable 'Thanks, Todd' to himself suggests he has lost all composure. Solid cringe escalation.

jokes.text: After a game ends, Abbi: 'We had that. That is a bullshit call.' / 'See you in the fucking parking lot.'
jokes.setup: Trey announced 'no losers' for the team games
jokes.punchline: Abbi is immediately disputing calls and threatening parking lot confrontations — full competitive violence-mode — having told Trey just five minutes ago she'd try to stay calm
jokes.explanation: Classic Broad City setup-payoff across a scene gap. Abbi's self-awareness has a half-life of about 90 seconds. 'See you in the fucking parking lot' to gym coworkers is a wonderfully disproportionate response.

jokes.text: After winning tug-of-war: 'Oh, yeah! Red team, you guys come and eat my ass one at a fucking time.'
jokes.setup: Abbi's team has just won tug-of-war
jokes.punchline: Victory trash talk escalates to a sexual/degradation instruction ('eat my ass one at a fucking time') — the 'one at a time' logistics implying she's done the math on capacity
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'one at a fucking time' is what makes this great — it's trash talk that has accidentally become a logistical directive, suggesting Abbi is so far gone she's now organized the ass-eating queue. Highly quotable, strong escalation of the all-caps Abbi arc.

jokes.text: 'Unh! Line up, come on. I'm selling tickets.' — then realizing people are staring — 'I was kidding, I was totally kidding around. You guys were great.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just told the losing team to 'eat my ass one at a time'
jokes.punchline: She escalates to selling tickets, clocks the horrified reactions, and attempts to pass the whole thing off as light-hearted banter — the snap-back to normal is funnier than the trash talk
jokes.explanation: The instant reset — 'I was kidding, I was totally kidding around. You guys were great.' — is a great Abbi beat. She goes 0 to 100 and back to 0 in four seconds, and the 'You guys were great' is tone-deaf praise that only makes things worse.

jokes.text: Ilana tweets '50% off vibrators at Vibeland' from the company account as her first tweet, tags it as 'Soloplay, bb's' and gets 69 notifications
jokes.setup: Ilana has been given control of the company's Twitter account
jokes.punchline: Her first company tweet is a vibrator sale ad; she gets 69 notifications (numerical joke); she celebrates with a fist pump
jokes.explanation: The 69 notifications is a knowing wink — the show is aware of the number's connotation and the vibrator subject makes it a clean double joke. 'Soloplay, bb's' as ad copy is good Ilana voice. Solid setup for the Mr. Hands escalation.

jokes.text: Victory cut — '♪ I tweet ♪' — mirrors the '♪ I shit ♪' motif
jokes.setup: The '♪ I shit ♪' jingle was established earlier
jokes.punchline: The same musical format now scores tweeting — equating social media activity with defecation in value and register
jokes.explanation: The running gag pays off beautifully here. The callback structure — same melody, same format — applied now to tweeting implicitly equates the two acts. It's also the moment where the structure of the gag reveals itself as a pattern, which makes both the original and this instance funnier retroactively. Earns its 8+ for structural elegance and economy.

jokes.text: Abbi's post-tug-of-war trash talk to 'Tomas': 'What now, Tomas, huh? Who's the hot commodity now, huh? It's me, yes!'
jokes.setup: Abbi has won another event
jokes.punchline: 'Hot commodity' is an odd term to use in a recreational gym competition — she's trash-talking using business language, which implies her competitive instinct has a strange flavor
jokes.explanation: Functional escalation moment in Abbi's competitive meltdown. 'Hot commodity' is an odd register for trash talk, which gives it a slight absurdist flavor. Not exceptional but consistent.

jokes.text: Ilana calls Abbi mid-competition and narrates her next tweet idea: 'Yankee Candle store, vanilla bean. BB&B, right when it opens. 8:00 a.m., you and the employees.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is riding the high of her 69-notification vibrator tweet and calls Abbi for her next idea
jokes.punchline: Her tweet concept is... vanilla bean Yankee Candle + Bed Bath & Beyond at opening + the employees, apparently as participants in whatever scenario she's imagining
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the unspoken implication — what exactly is happening with the BB&B employees at 8am? The 'Oh, man, I love that' response from Abbi (who is supposed to be alarmed) and then Ilana's 'Oh fuck' as she sees something is a nice beat.

jokes.text: Ilana tells Abbi: 'You are all-caps Abbi right now. I need you to be case sensitive.'
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives at the Soulstice Games and confronts competitive Abbi
jokes.punchline: 'Case sensitive' repurposes a computer term as a personality instruction — Abbi being lowercase, nuanced, context-aware vs. ALL CAPS SCREAMING — it's a precise and original coinage for Abbi's competitive mode
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode and one of the show's best character coinages. 'All-caps Abbi' and 'be case sensitive' repurpose text-formatting language as personality modulation — it's elegant, original, character-specific (only Ilana's brain works this way), perfectly economical, and instantly quotable. It works as a metaphor (ALL CAPS = yelling, unmodulated, no nuance), as character assessment, and as instruction. This is exactly the kind of joke that becomes part of a show's vocabulary.

jokes.text: Gemma challenges Abbi to the pugil-stick event. Abbi: 'Thanks so much... Not!'
jokes.setup: Trey has been about to run the event when Gemma challenges Abbi directly
jokes.punchline: 'Thanks so much... Not!' — the Wayne's World 'not' construction, deployed by Abbi completely sincerely in 2014, suggesting either retro-irony or genuine earnest use
jokes.explanation: The 'Not!' construction is deliberately dated — it's funny because all-caps Abbi has apparently regressed to early-90s trash talk syntax. The episode treats it as completely earnest, which is funnier than if it were a knowing reference.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I am gonna knock your big swangin' titties into next Tuesday.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Gemma are about to fight in the pugil-stick event
jokes.punchline: Abbi's trash talk combines body-shaming, anatomical specificity ('swangin' titties'), and the vintage 'into next Tuesday' phrase in a way that is both aggressive and weirdly affectionate-sounding
jokes.explanation: Extremely quotable all-caps Abbi line. 'Swangin' titties into next Tuesday' is a memorable compound of two different clichés that somehow works. The body specificity makes it genuinely funny — it's not generic trash talk, it's targeted. High impact because of delivery and absurdity.

jokes.text: Gemma: 'I'm gonna wipe the floor with you, but it looks like you already did that, cleaner!'
jokes.setup: Abbi has delivered a strong trash-talk line; Gemma retaliates
jokes.punchline: Gemma goes for a class-based insult (calling Abbi 'cleaner' — implying she cleans floors for a living), grafted onto the 'wipe the floor' idiom
jokes.explanation: The wordplay construction ('wipe the floor... you already did that, cleaner') is functional but the joke relies on a class-based put-down that isn't particularly inspired. It's funny because it's Abbi's actual job, but the setup telegraphs the punchline clearly. The 'Not!' energy from Abbi's side is better.

jokes.text: Abbi immediately panics and tries to walk back everything: 'No, I'm kidding, you guys, I'm kidding, come on! I'm Kidding!'
jokes.setup: Abbi has said 'swangin' titties into next Tuesday' and is now fully aware she's crossed a line
jokes.punchline: The sprint from aggression to desperately overclaiming 'kidding' is the joke — she's clearly not kidding and everyone knows it
jokes.explanation: A reliable Abbi beat — the snap-back to social acceptability that fools no one. 'I'm Kidding!' escalating in emphasis is good, but this is the third or fourth iteration of the same beat in this episode.

jokes.text: Trey tells Abbi she doesn't need to do steroids to be cool — then immediately walks her through a half-dose anabolic regimen anyway, noting 'it does make your balls shrivel, but in your case, it's gonna make your boobs rock hard, which is a bless.'
jokes.setup: Trey wants to check in with Abbi about the competitive meltdown
jokes.punchline: He opens with a message of acceptance/no-steroids positivity, then immediately pivots to detailed steroid instructions on the assumption she might still want them — 'it's gonna make your boobs rock hard, which is a bless' as a selling point
jokes.explanation: Perfect Trey joke. The structure is: [affirming non-steroid message] → [detailed steroid instructions]. The pivot destroys the initial message, revealing Trey's worldview: steroids are fine, he just wants you to feel okay either way. 'Which is a bless' (not 'a blessing,' just 'a bless') is an original and specific verbal tic. 'Just a tip' as a closing is the perfect straight-faced capper.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'This tweet will be my Ms. 'Olland's Opus.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has an idea for a great tweet
jokes.punchline: She invokes 'Mr. Holland's Opus' (the 1995 Richard Dreyfuss movie about a music teacher's legacy) as the benchmark for her tweet's importance — missing both the movie's name AND its cultural significance
jokes.explanation: The gender-swap ('Ms. 'Olland') combined with the grandiose use of a mildly obscure movie title for a corporate tweet is very Ilana. It's also foreshadowing — her 'opus' tweet turns out to be a bestiality video, which makes the Mr. Holland reference retroactively perfect.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I'm gonna sprint home right now.' Abbi: 'To Queens?' Ilana: 'Yeah, you want to race me?' Abbi: 'I'm not going to Queens...'
jokes.setup: Ilana is leaving to tweet her opus from home
jokes.punchline: Ilana, apparently genuinely, offers to race Abbi to Queens — forgetting that Abbi has no reason to go to Queens — and Abbi declines so gently it's almost sad
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Abbi's bewildered and gentle non-participation: 'I'm not going to Queens.' A small character moment but well-executed.

jokes.text: Nicole is singing a hymn ('Joyful, Joyful') to herself at her desk immediately after the Carlton meeting — then breaks the song to call out 'Nicole!' — then keeps singing
jokes.setup: Transitioning from the chaos of the Ilana/Carlton bathroom sequence
jokes.punchline: Nicole has apparently self-soothed through the whole ordeal by listening to a gospel hymn, then breaks her trance to respond to her name, then returns to the hymn
jokes.explanation: A good visual/tonal gag — the gospel music as stress response in an office setting is funny, and the interruption-and-return structure implies she's been doing this for some time. The show playing this completely straight makes it better.

jokes.text: Todd: 'You tweeted an extremely graphic bestiality video.' Ilana: 'Todd, it's Mr. Hands. It's a staple of early digital culture. Talk about viral. Google it.'
jokes.setup: Todd is firing Ilana for the tweet
jokes.punchline: Ilana has tweeted 'Mr. Hands' — the actual infamous early-internet bestiality video — and presents it as a serious viral content play, recommending Todd Google it
jokes.explanation: Exceptional payoff to the Salad Fingers/early digital culture thread from the Carlton meeting. Ilana's entire internet-culture expertise thesis has been building to this: her 'opus' tweet is Mr. Hands, framed as a serious content strategy. 'It's a staple of early digital culture. Talk about viral. Google it.' is perfect — the cheerful, expert tone while recommending Todd Google one of the internet's most notorious videos. High impact because of commitment and escalation. The callback to the viral conversation with Carlton makes this the episode's big joke payoff.

jokes.text: Ilana corrects the bestiality record: 'And if you did your homework, Todd, you would know that the horse was fucking the guy. It's chill!'
jokes.setup: Todd calls it a PR nightmare for the bestiality content
jokes.punchline: Ilana's mitigating argument is not 'it wasn't what it looked like' but rather a precise factual correction about the direction of the sex act — and uses 'it's chill' as if this distinction resolves everything
jokes.explanation: The best pure joke in the episode, possibly the season. The logic is inverted: Ilana isn't defending against the accusation — she's accepting it and issuing a fact-check. The fact-check is: the horse was the penetrating party, which in Ilana's ethical framework constitutes important context that makes it 'chill.' The leap of reasoning (passive human victim = less bad) deployed with total confidence is a perfect dark-comedy construction. 'It's chill!' as the landing is exactly right. This is close to a cultural touchstone for the show.

jokes.text: The overlapping firing argument where both Todd and Ilana are simultaneously making competing cases to an empty room, talking over each other: 'I have put my blood, sweat... / You make all the other employees uncomfortable / ...And farts into this company.'
jokes.setup: Todd is firing Ilana; she's refusing to be fired and listing her contributions
jokes.punchline: Ilana's list of personal sacrifices to the company climaxes with 'farts' — 'blood, sweat, and farts' as a corruption of the idiom — while Todd's counter-argument continues underneath
jokes.explanation: 'Blood, sweat, and farts' is a good corruption of a cliché delivered with full sincerity in a crossfire scene. The simultaneous-talking structure serves the chaos of the moment. The structural joke (both arguing points that don't engage with each other) mirrors their working relationship.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I am strong, and you are fired.'
jokes.setup: Todd has told Ilana she is fired
jokes.punchline: Ilana flips the power dynamic by firing Todd in response — 'I am strong, and you are fired' — as if she has the authority and he has been the problem
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. The two clauses ('I am strong' / 'you are fired') are in the same grammatical structure which makes the second clause feel like a logical conclusion of the first. The confidence is total. This is a very quotable Ilana power-move line.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'This is just the white man holding on to the last nugget of his power.' / 'You white dudes are figuring out that the jig is fucking up!'
jokes.setup: Todd has confirmed her firing
jokes.punchline: Ilana frames being fired for tweeting a bestiality video from a company account as a structural racism issue — 'the jig is fucking up' (not 'the jig is up') is an additional verbal malfunction
jokes.explanation: Ilana's tendency to apply progressive political frameworks to situations where they manifestly don't apply (being fired for bestiality content) is one of her recurring character comedic traits. 'The jig is fucking up' as a mangled version of 'the jig is up' is a nice bonus error that plays perfectly in context — delivered with the same certainty as everything else.

jokes.text: 'I'm gonna go talk to Mom!' — then Todd's 'Oh, you are absolutely fired, effective immediately.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is threatening further action
jokes.punchline: Her nuclear option is 'I'm gonna go talk to Mom' — implying Todd is or is related to her mother in some way, or that 'Mom' is a superior she'll escalate to — which lands as childlike and absurd. Todd firing her 'effectively immediately' implies there was previously some ambiguity.
jokes.explanation: The 'I'm gonna go talk to Mom' threat is delightfully unspecified — whose mom? Is Todd related to the Wexlers? Does Ilana have a mom-connected HR escalation pathway? The deliberate ambiguity is funny. Todd's 'absolutely fired, effective immediately' implying previous firings were non-immediate is a good straight-man button.

jokes.text: Ilana tells Todd: 'You're right, it's time to change. And I figured it out. I want to be... in you.'
jokes.setup: Todd says 'I need you to leave'
jokes.punchline: Ilana appears to have a moment of genuine reflection — 'You're right, it's time to change' — then ends on 'I want to be... in you' as her resolution
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — the reflective pause before 'in you' sets up a fake resolution beat. Ilana's casual delivery of what is essentially a sexual proposition as a life-change pivot is perfect character work. The pause before 'in you' (suggested by the ellipsis) is the key structural element.

jokes.text: Ilana's farewell tour: addressing individual coworkers by their physical descriptors — 'White Guy Number Seven,' 'White Guy Number Three' (who started the man bun trend), 'Adult Braces' (who corrects her that he got them off seven months ago)
jokes.setup: Ilana is doing a dramatic goodbye speech to her coworkers
jokes.punchline: She has apparently never learned most of her coworkers' names and addresses them by physical descriptor; 'Adult Braces' has been free of braces for seven months and is clearly used to being called this
jokes.explanation: Excellent sustained bit. The detail that 'Adult Braces' got them off seven months ago and is still being called that — and doesn't really object — implies Ilana has been calling him 'Adult Braces' for months post-removal, and he has resigned himself to it. 'Would you rather I go back to Only Black Guy?' is an incredible moment of both Ilana self-awareness and systemic comedy. The escalation from White Guy Seven to White Guy Three to Adult Braces to the conclusion builds well.

jokes.text: Employee corrects: 'I got those off seven months ago.' Ilana: 'Would you rather I go back to Only Black Guy?'
jokes.setup: Adult Braces has clarified he no longer has braces
jokes.punchline: Ilana reveals her previous nickname for him was 'Only Black Guy' — and frames keeping 'Adult Braces' as the enlightened option
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect joke. The structure is: correction → worse revelation → the correction is now seen as progress. 'Would you rather I go back to Only Black Guy?' treats a racial identifier as a previous bad habit she upgraded from — and the employee says 'Adult Braces is fine,' which means he prefers the braces nickname. The social dynamics compressed into 8 words are remarkable. It's dark, it's precise, it's only Ilana, and the employee's resigned acceptance makes it land harder. One of the episode's two or three best moments.

jokes.text: Ilana leaves the office fish behind 'because I really do believe in corporate morale.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is collecting her things and heading for the door
jokes.punchline: She leaves her personal fish as a gift/gesture — framed as a principled organizational commitment, not a practical decision about pet transport
jokes.explanation: The corporate morale justification for abandoning a fish is a good Ilana rationalization joke. The juxtaposition of 'I really do believe' (sincere conviction language) with 'corporate morale' (bureaucratic language) as a reason to leave a pet is good economy.

jokes.text: Ilana leaves her desk plant at the office: 'I'm leaving him here because I really do believe in corporate morale.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been fired and is gathering her things
jokes.punchline: She sacrifices her plant — presumably sentimentally meaningful — as an institutional contribution, invoking 'corporate morale' as the reason, framing a plant abandonment as an act of organizational citizenship
jokes.explanation: Sweet and absurd. Ilana's genuine belief in corporate morale as a principle — despite having just tweeted horse pornography — is a nice character contradiction. The plant as institutional sacrifice is oddly touching.

jokes.text: 'Day 511... It ends. I'm free. You know what? I'm gonna unfollow Deals, Deals, Deals.'
jokes.setup: Post-firing; Ilana is walking with Abbi
jokes.punchline: 'Day 511' implies she has been counting the days of her employment like a prison sentence — and her first act of liberation is unfollowing on social media
jokes.explanation: 'Day 511' is good — it's a very specific number implying a daily countdown she's been running. The anti-climax of unfollowing as the freedom gesture is perfect.

jokes.text: The company's Twitter apology tweet: 'We sincerely apologize for our last tweet. It was posted by a former employee who suffers from mental health issues.' Ilana: 'We all do.'
jokes.setup: Abbi reads the company's damage-control tweet to Ilana
jokes.punchline: The mental health framing in the apology is corporate spin; Ilana's 'We all do' is a sincere agreement that accidentally validates their framing while also being broadly true
jokes.explanation: 'We all do' is good — it takes the company's punching-down spin and converts it into a moment of solidarity/universality. The best possible response in two words.

jokes.text: Tweet continues: 'We do not promote bestiality, but we still promote colonics.'
jokes.setup: The company is clarifying its brand values in the apology tweet
jokes.punchline: The damage-control tweet pivots from anti-bestiality disclaimer to colonic promotion — the tonal continuity ('but we still promote colonics') treats these as comparable categories of behavior requiring clarification
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural joke. The PR tweet's need to clarify 'we do not promote bestiality BUT we still promote colonics' is a perfect comedy of corporate voice — the pivot uses the same conjunction ('but') that normally separates two equally weighted propositions. The colonic callback also ties back to the episode's recurring scatological sting ('I shit'). Very well constructed.

jokes.text: Ilana insists: 'The horse was fucking the guy. It's honestly not that bad.' / 'It's not as bad as if a human was fucking the animal. That's all. That's all.' / 'I think everyone agrees, on that. Right?'
jokes.setup: The company tweet has restated the bestiality scandal
jokes.punchline: Ilana's continued defense — in the street, to Abbi, now with 'I think everyone agrees, on that. Right?' seeking external validation for her ethical hierarchy — extends and slightly deflates the earlier Mr. Hands joke
jokes.explanation: This is a callback/extension of the earlier Mr. Hands joke — slightly diminishing returns as the premise is being re-explained, but 'I think everyone agrees, on that. Right?' seeking street consensus is funny. The logic is now stated more explicitly which makes it less surprising than the first delivery.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'That was the worst job in the world, don't even worry about it.' Ilana: 'I know, fuck them.' Abbi: 'Let's just forget all our flaws, 'cause today was a total fluke.' Ilana: 'Yeah, well, I wish I could forget, like, my hunger for competition, but it's like... It's online, so, like, I can't.'
jokes.setup: The two are decompressing after their respective bad days; Abbi proposes mutual absolution
jokes.punchline: Ilana reveals that she can't forget her competitive hunger because 'it's online' — implying the Red Rover results or some equivalent competitive record exists on the internet as evidence
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the episode's closing sequence. 'It's online, so I can't' as a reason forgetting is impossible is a funny modern-era obstacle to emotional growth. It also seeds the payoff.

jokes.text: Abbi responds with 'Awesome.' to Ilana's bestiality moral framework
jokes.setup: Ilana has just laid out her ethical position on Mr. Hands
jokes.punchline: 'Awesome.' — a single word of complete non-engagement, the most casual possible response to the most inappropriate content
jokes.explanation: One word, perfect timing. Abbi's complete acceptance — not even 'okay' or 'sure,' but the positive 'Awesome' — is the ideal reaction beat. It normalizes everything Ilana just said.

jokes.text: Online Red Rover: 'Red Rover, Red Rover, send Abbi right over!' — Abbi charges through a digital barrier — 'Oh, she's biting me!' / 'Arms up! Breathe, breathe... Shh...' / 'She's heading for the woods! She's a climber!'
jokes.setup: Abbi is playing online Red Rover — presumably on a phone or computer — but has apparently done something physically
jokes.punchline: Abbi has gone full feral — biting, running for the woods, being restrained by Ilana — in what appears to have started as a digital game and become a physical rampage
jokes.explanation: The episode's closing sequence pays off every comedic thread simultaneously — the Camp Kweebec Red Rover mythology, all-caps Abbi, the competitive meltdown arc, and Ilana's protective best-friend role. The cascade of details ('she's biting me,' 'she's a climber,' 'she's heading for the woods') suggests Abbi has fully become feral and Ilana is stage-managing crowd control. The visual gag of whatever Abbi is actually doing (presumably charging through a crowd after seeing her name called in a phone game) is inferred beautifully. This is a high-craft closing gag.

jokes.text: The episode ends with Abbi playing online Red Rover and physically attacking someone through her phone screen — 'She's biting me! She's heading for the woods! She's a climber!'
jokes.setup: Abbi has found online Red Rover; Ilana has called 'Red Rover, Red Rover, send Abbi right over!'
jokes.punchline: Abbi physically charges through whatever obstacle is in her way — the game has caused an actual physical altercation — ending with Ilana and others pursuing her into the woods as she climbs
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode closer. The running joke pays off maximally — Abbi charges, bites someone, and literally flees into the woods, all over an online children's game. 'She's heading for the woods! She's a climber!' as the final image is absurdist enough to be memorable. All the episode's established details — Camp Kweebec, all-caps Abbi, the Tonya Harding warning — collapse into this single feral moment. Solid payoff for a well-constructed A-plot.
